notes: Tested the analog against three hostile lenses. (1) 'This will be used to chain engineers to bad jobs' — the falsifiability paragraph explicitly excludes this: 'A miserable role one has accepted but is not actively executing is not the verse. A duty whose handover would take a week and the team is willing to absorb is not the verse.' (2) 'No engineer should ever feel they cannot quit' — the counter_example handles the case of weaponised un-replaceability where the team refused to absorb knowledge: the engineer's exit there is the system's overdue correction. (3) 'The named tools (custom Postgres logical-replication plugin, WAL-shipping, on-call runbook) are decorative' — false, these are real artifacts of a database-as-a-service company's failure-mode architecture. The doctrinal claim — that mid-execution abandonment of an actually un-replaceable role is a real and distinct category of failure — is engineering-true, not just verse-pretty. The implication offers a real alternative shape (delayed start, transition runbook, paid contracting bridge). PASS.
